Q12: ABA Problem Your Answer: 🟡 Partial. Let me explain it more clearly: ABA happens when: Thread T1 reads value A from a location. Thread T2 modifies it from A → B → A. T1 does CAS on A — and it succeeds! But the state is not the same, it was temporarily changed. ✅ To mitigate: Use a version number with pointer (tagged pointer). Use std::atomic<std::uintptr_t> or std::atomic<std::pair<ptr, tag>> Or use hazard pointers / epoch-based reclamation. Q13: Why compare_exchange_weak may fail spuriously

htop: Provides real-time CPU usage per core and per thread (toggle F5 for tree view). You can set thread names in your ROS 2 node (using pthread_setname_np) to identify threads easily. Allows sorting by CPU%, memory, etc., to identify heavy threads. perf: perf stat -p <pid> gathers aggregate stats (cycles, instructions, cache misses). perf top -p <pid> gives real-time CPU hotspots in your process. perf record -p <pid> -g -- sleep 10 captures call graph samples for CPU profiling. 5) Deep dive: Thread affinity and real-time scheduling (memory-focused) 🔸 Thread Affinity Means pinning a thread to a specific CPU core. Avoids migrating thread between cores → avoids cold cache. Without affinity: OS can move your thread around → bad cache locality. Thread might get cache misses more often. With affinity: Same core = hot L1/L2 cache → faster memory access. No TLB flushes, better branch prediction too. Set affinity via pthread_setaffinity_np() or sched_setaffinity() in Linux. 🔸 R

🔸 Regular BST: A Binary Search Tree (BST) stores keys such that: Left child < parent < Right child It can become unbalanced, leading to O(n) time operations in the worst case. 🔸 Red-Black Tree (used in std::map): A self-balancing BST with the following guarantees: Each node is colored red or black No two red nodes in a row Every path from root to leaf has the same number of black nodes Can you explain a bit on unbalanced BST and No two red nodes in a row Every path from root to leaf has the same

3. Cache Misses Three Types: Compulsory Miss: First access to data. Capacity Miss: Cache is full, something must be evicted. Conflict Miss: Two data items map to same cache set. 💣 Example: Capacity Miss cpp Copy Edit int arr[1000000]; for (int i = 0; i < 1000000; i += 16) { arr[i]++; } If array size exceeds cache size, items get evicted before reuse → constant reloading from RAM = cache miss storm. 4. Multi-threaded Cache Contention (Single Core) Let’s say you have a single-core CPU and two threads. The core executes one thread at a time. They share the L1/L2 cache. If both threads operate on different data, that’s fine. If both operate on data in the same cache line, they cause false sharing (as shown above). 1. What are your most commonly used Git commands in daily development? Expected Answer: I commonly use: git status and git diff to check the current state. git pull to sync with the remote. git add, git commit and git push for changes. git log to inspect history. git checkout -b <branch> and git merge/rebase for branching. git stash when I need to temporarily save changes. For debugging history, I use git blame, git bisect, and git reflog. Can you explain each of them with practical small code,
git status to check the current changed files compared to head or server? git diff is used to check the current changes in each file with respect to head?

git diff -> compares not staged changes means(not added for commit ) with HEAD. It doesn't include added changes including untracked files git diff --cached or git diff -- staged -> These only compare staged changes (added changes) with current head git diff HEAD -> compare both staged and non-staged changes. Are these statements correct?
what if I stage few files out of total -> then what git diff do, does it compare to the head or both head and staged file
staging area -> staged files + head right?
Command Compares git diff Working Directory ↔ Staging Area here staging area is what, as non staging file will automatically diff with HEAD files

Case B: File is modified and staged once, then modified again You git add file.cpp (staging area now has snapshot B). You edit again (working directory has version C). Now git diff compares: Working Directory (C) ↔ Staging Area (B) ✅ You won’t see changes relative to HEAD (A) anymore. That is what I am saying File is already staged on top of head, SO it is now automatically comparing with staged file which already includes HEad file in this context

1. ✅ How does the particle filter work? Answer: AMCL uses a particle filter, where each particle represents a possible robot pose (x, y, θ). Steps: Initialization: Distribute particles across map (uniform or from user pose). Prediction: Use odometry to move particles based on robot’s motion. Update: Compare sensor data (LIDAR) for each particle against the map. Calculate likelihood that a particle's pose explains the scan. Weighting: Assign higher weights to particles that match better. 12. What are zombie and orphan processes? How do you handle them? 👻 Zombie Process: A child process that has exited, but parent hasn’t wait()-ed for it. It stays in the process table (defunct). ps aux | grep Z ✅ How to prevent: signal(SIGCHLD, SIG_IGN); // Automatically reaps children 🧒 Orphan Process: A process whose parent has exited. Gets reparented to init (PID 1). Real-life debugging: If you saw many <defunct> processes in your EHMI system, it means you're missing wait() in your multitaskin

18. Did you ever write your own memory pool or fixed-size allocator? Why? ✅ In performance-critical systems (like slot games or EHMI), memory pools help avoid: Repeated malloc/free overhead Fragmentation Allocation latency Memory Pool: Pre-allocate a large block of memory. Hand out fixed-size chunks. Simple Example: char pool[1024]; void* allocate(size_t size) { static size_t offset = 0; void* ptr = &pool[offset]; offset += size; return ptr; } ✅ In your case: You likely used preallocated buffers

21. What is context switching and how expensive is it in embedded devices? Context Switching: When the CPU switches from one task/process/thread to another. Saves current task state and loads the next. How AMCL works: Maintains a cloud of particles, each representing a possible robot pose. Each particle has a weight representing the likelihood of that pose. When new sensor data arrives (LIDAR scan), AMCL evaluates how well each particle’s pose matches the map. Particles that match the sensor data better get higher weights. ✅ 3) How Do Nodes Communicate Across Machines? Your version: Via topic and services. Refined Answer: Nodes on different machines communicate using ROS 2 middleware (DDS), which handles discovery and transport of messages over the network. Topics and services can work transparently across machines as long as they are on the same ROS 2 domain and network. The discovery uses UDP multicast by default, and communication can happen over UDP or TCP depending on QoS. Bonus: Mention environment variable

1) Topic is a asynchronous call where one or many node can publish it and many node can subscribe it at a time and change the behaviour accordingly. It is unidirectional. Topic is important key of ROS2 in order to communicate between the nodes and of course most used one also. On the other hand service is a synchronous call where one node can send the service to another node and can wait for the result synchronously. 2) Servie over topic is used when we need immediate result in order to proceed.

I need your help we are going to create a project related to total vehicle management. We need to break down requirements in the initial phase. One of my team member have created few high level requirements for the project. I need your help to break them down for me and add some valuable inputs from your side also. Our top level requirement is we need to create a system where we can communicate with the vehicles remotely and perform all the necessary tasks related to the management. This project

In Formik's Field component, many properties are automatically managed by Formik, and you do not need to provide them explicitly. These properties include: value: Description: The current value of the field is managed by Formik based on the form state, so you do not need to pass it explicitly. onChange: Description: Formik handles the onChange event for you, updating the field's value in the form state. onBlur: Description: Formik automatically manages the onBlur event to mark the field as touch
